blurb: DeepMind's recent output straddles UK government partnership and frontier-safety research: an AI planning-decisions prototype built with UK government, plus an 'AI Control Roadmap' for securing AI agents and a $10M multi-agent-safety funding call.

publications.title: Introducing computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash
publications.summary: DeepMind ships 'computer use' (agentic UI control) in Gemini 3.5 Flash. Capability news rather than policy, but policy-adjacent: it extends the agentic-systems frontier DeepMind has been framing as its own safety/governance sub-domain (cf. its Jun-16 AI Control Roadmap). Worth watching whether agent-control capabilities and agent-security policy move in step.

publications.title: Unlocking UK house-building with AI-accelerated planning
publications.summary: DeepMind and the UK government announce a prototype aimed at faster housing/planning decisions. London-relevant: this is a frontier lab embedding directly into a UK government delivery priority (planning reform), the kind of public-sector partnership that shapes a lab's domestic policy standing.

publications.title: Securing the future of AI agents
publications.summary: Introduces an 'AI Control Roadmap' combining traditional safeguards with real-time monitoring to secure internal agent systems. Agent security is becoming its own policy sub-domain; worth watching whether this language shows up in DeepMind's regulatory engagement.

publications.title: Investing in multi-agent AI safety research
publications.summary: DeepMind and partners announce a $10M funding call for multi-agent safety research. Funding-the-field move; signals where the lab thinks the next safety frontier sits.

notes: blogRssUrl https://deepmind.google/blog/rss.xml confirmed working (note its <atom:link self> is a placeholder example.com URL, but item links are valid).

controversies.title: ICO ruling: Royal Free–DeepMind 1.6m-patient data deal breached the Data Protection Act
controversies.summary: In 2015 the Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust gave Google DeepMind access to the health records of around 1.6 million patients to build Streams, an app to flag acute kidney injury. Following a year-long investigation, in July 2017 the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) concluded the Trust had failed to comply with the Data Protection Act: sensitive health data was shared without patients' knowledge or consent and with no opt-out, and the Trust should have been far more transparent about how the information was used. The case became a landmark 'cautionary tale' on health-data partnerships in the algorithmic age and later spawned a representative (class-action style) claim over the data use. Facts are drawn from the ICO's own public record.

news.title: Powering the future of robotics in Europe
news.summary: DeepMind 'Company'-category announcement on its European robotics push. Activity/stage signal; low policy content.
